lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Herr Frege, we have become quite adept at reasoning with propositions. Yet something continues to bother me.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: And what might that be?

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Our propositions are sealed boxes. I may call one $P$, another $Q$, but logic never asks what lies inside them.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Quite right. To propositional logic, 'Socrates is mortal' and 'The moon is made of cheese' are equally indivisible.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Yet surely they have structure.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Indeed they do. Let us open the box.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Then 'Socrates is mortal' is no longer a single proposition?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Rather, it consists of a [term|term]—Socrates—and a property—being mortal.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So the property is incomplete until a term fills its place?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Precisely. Such an incomplete statement is called a **[predicate|predicate]**.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And we usually write it as something like $M(x)$?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly. Here $M$ means 'is mortal,' while $x$ is a [variable|variable] representing an arbitrary term. In our notation, that is `(App M x)`.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Then if I replace $x$ with Socrates, I recover an ordinary proposition.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Indeed. We say that we [application|apply] the predicate to a term.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Can predicates have more than one missing place?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: At first glance, yes. We write $L(x, y)$ for 'x loves y.'

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So predicates may take multiple arguments.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: That is the standard notation. But I dislike it.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Why?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Because it is not uniform. It treats predicates as if they had different [arity|arities], and that forces us to add meta-level rules describing how each position behaves.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And that is a problem?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Yes. I want a system where every predicate behaves the same way: a single input, a single output. Nothing more.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Then how do you express relations like 'loves' without breaking uniformity?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: I do not yet know.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: You do not need to break it.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And you are?

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Haskell Curry. I make logic run.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Interessant.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Take $L(x, y)$. Instead of two inputs, think of it as: $L(x)$ first, which produces a new function waiting for $y$.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So $L(x)$ returns not a proposition, but the predicate 'x loves _'?

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Yep.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: A function that returns another function… interesting.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Exactly. Nothing special is required for multiple arguments. Only repetition of the same rule.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: You already have the notation: `(App L x)` gives you a new predicate, and `(App (App L x) y)` completes the application. Or just `(App L x y)` — the machine [syntactic-sugar|desugars] it for you. No new machinery needed.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Oh and one more thing. You'll want to introduce fresh terms the way you introduce assumptions. The command is `(fix x)` — gives you $\{x\} \vdash x$.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: And now I must return to my own abstractions. Good luck with yours.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: He appeared, changed the shape of our thinking, and left just as quickly.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: But wait — `(fix x)`. He said it was like assuming a term. I understand assuming a proposition: we pretend $P$ is true and see what follows. But what does it mean to *assume a variable*?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: An excellent question. So far we have used the [context|context] only to hold truths — statements we borrow temporarily. But the context can hold more.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: More?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: It can hold an inventory of things. Objects we have named. When you write `(fix x)`, you are not claiming $x$ is true — you are simply declaring that $x$ exists as a thing we may speak about.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So the context collects both what is true and what exists?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Precisely. Think of it as a workshop. On the left side of $\vdash$, we keep assumptions about truth — $P$, $Q \to R$ — and also the names of objects we have introduced — $x$, $y$. On the right, we build what follows.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And once $x$ is in the workshop, we can feed it to a predicate — `(App P x)` — and reason about the result.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Yes. You see now why Curry mentioned `(fix x)` right after application. The two belong together: introduce a thing, then apply predicates to it.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So `(assume P)` borrows a truth. `(fix x)` borrows an object. Both go into the context, both are discharged later.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: And that uniformity is precisely what makes the system elegant.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Curious. Every predicate now follows exactly the same rule. Every variable introduction follows the same pattern. The context is no longer just a list of borrowed truths — it is a full inventory of our reasoning.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: And now that predicates and terms are uniform, a deeper question opens.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Which is?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: We can now speak of properties of individual objects. But how do we make assertions about **all** objects, or merely **some**?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: For that, we require an entirely new idea.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: A new connective?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Not quite. We must speak about all possible terms.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: That sounds ambitious.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Suppose I wish to say, 'Every human is mortal.' Rather than naming every human individually, I introduce a temporary variable, $x$.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Much like algebra.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly. I then declare that no matter which object $x$ denotes, if it is human, then it is mortal.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And this declaration has its own notation?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Indeed. We write the **[universal-quantifier|universal quantifier]**, $\forall x$.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Thus $\forall x.\ Human(x) \to Mortal(x)$ means that every human is mortal.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Precisely. And the proof rules are simpler than they sound. Let me show you.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Go on.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Suppose from nothing but `(fix x)`, we prove `(-> (App Human x) (App Mortal x))` — 'if x is human, x is mortal.'

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: But we know nothing about x. It's just an arbitrary person.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly! That is the point. Because the proof works for a completely arbitrary $x$, it works for **anyone**. At that moment, we may write `(forall-intro x 3)` — 'from step 3, generalise to all $x$'.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So `(forall-intro ...)` says: I proved this for an arbitrary thing, therefore it holds for every thing.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: And the reverse is even simpler. Say step 1 proves `(forall x (-> (App Human x) (App Mortal x)))` — all humans are mortal.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Now I wish to apply that to Socrates.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Then write `(forall-elim 1 socrates)`. The command strips off the $\forall x$ and substitutes `socrates` for $x$. You get `(-> (App Human socrates) (App Mortal socrates))` — if Socrates is human, Socrates is mortal.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So `(forall-elim ...)` is just substitution. What holds for all holds for the one.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Precisely. And now the existential.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: The universal speaks of all. The existential speaks of some.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Yes — **[existential-quantifier|existential quantifier]** $\exists x$. Where $\forall x. (\to Human(x) Mortal(x))$ means every human is mortal, $\exists x. Mortal(x)$ means merely that at least one mortal exists.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: It promises existence without naming who.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly. And like the universal, it comes with an introduction rule and an elimination rule.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: From one mortal, I want to say 'something is mortal.'

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly. Write `(exists-intro 2 socrates x)`. You give the step with the example, the term that serves as your evidence, and a fresh variable name. The result is `(exists x (App Mortal x))` — there exists something mortal.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So `(exists-intro ...)` says: here is a concrete example, therefore something with this property exists.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: That sounds straightforward. But the elimination rule seems harder.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Only in description, not in use. Let me walk through it.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Please.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Step 3 proves `(exists x (App P x))` — 'something has property P.' But which thing? We don't know.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So we cannot use the existential directly — we don't know who the witness is.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Correct. So we open a temporary witness. We write `(fix y)` — here is a fresh name, $y$, standing in for the unknown thing. Then we assume $y$ has the property: `(App P y)`. Under that assumption, we prove some conclusion — call it $\psi$.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: And if we can reach $\psi$ from that assumption?

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Then `(exists-elim 3 5 y)` says: the existential at step 3, together with the subproof at step 5 that used witness $y$, yields $\psi$. The critical rule: $y$ must not appear free in $\psi$ or any other assumption. It was only a stand-in.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Exactly. And that is why the four quantifier rules are all variations on the same idea: a variable enters the context, serves its purpose, and is discharged.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: These variables seem rather like assumptions in a proof. They appear, serve their purpose, and disappear.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: An excellent analogy. A variable has meaning only within the region governed by its [quantifier|quantifier].

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Outside that region, it is meaningless.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: Precisely. We say that the quantifier creates a **[bound-variable|bound variable]**.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So first-order logic is no longer merely about combining propositions.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: No. It is about reasoning directly about objects, their properties, and the relations between them.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: We have opened the box. On a related note, Plato's core reasoning engine will now switch from the propositional logic (PL) axiom set to first-order logic (FOL). This unlocks inference rules for quantifiers, variables, and predicates.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Propositional logic will return in a later section. I will not spoil it for you — you'll have to explore that yourself.

lines.speaker: Frege
lines.text: And once one can reason about the contents of propositions, logic becomes powerful enough to describe vast portions of mathematics itself.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Then I suspect our journey has only just begun.
